nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; benthic and benthopelagic; found at depths of 1–40 m, commonly 1–15 m. Adults tend to occupy coral reef slopes and lagoons; juveniles tend to favor shallow branching corals and seagrass meadows.

diet.notes: Has been documented consuming primarily fish (75–95% of diet) and some crustaceans in the wild. In captivity, has been reported to accept large meaty foods including krill, raw shrimp, squid, clam, mussel, and small feeder fish. Due to a relatively low metabolic rate, generally advised to feed every other day rather than daily. Tends to be an ambush predator, spending time hiding and waiting for prey.

summary: The Blue Dot Grouper (Cephalopholis argus) is a large, aggressive Indo-Pacific predator reaching up to 60 cm, generally advised for experienced hobbyists with very large tanks. Known for brilliant blue-spotted patterning.
careNotes:
The Blue Dot Grouper is a robust ambush predator that tends to spend much of its time near the substrate, hiding in caves or under overhangs and watching for prey. It has been known to consume any fish or crustacean small enough to fit in its mouth, so tankmate selection requires careful consideration — generally only large, semi-aggressive to aggressive fish of comparable or larger size are suitable companions.

This species produces significant biological waste, so a strong biological filtration system is generally advised. Live rock with abundant large caves and crevices is recommended to support its natural behaviors.

Regarding tank size: sources disagreed significantly — LiveAquaria recommends 250 gallons (946 L), AquariumDomain recommends 125 gallons (473 L), and BlueZoo Aquatics recommends 100 gallons (379 L). The averaged value of ~600 L (approximately 158 gallons) has been recorded here; given this species can reach 60 cm and is an active predator, the larger recommendations may be more appropriate for long-term welfare. Reviewer should assess.

Temperature ranges also showed moderate disagreement: LiveAquaria suggests 22.2–25.6°C (72–78°F) while AquariumDomain suggests 23.3–27.8°C (74–82°F) and FishBase data indicates 24–28°C. The averaged range of 23–27°C is used here.

Regarding adult size: aquarium trade sources typically cite 16 inches (40.6 cm) as the typical captive size, while FishBase and scientific sources document a maximum total length of 60 cm. The range 30–60 cm is used here to reflect both typical captive and potential maximum sizes.

This species is a protogynous hermaphrodite — individuals typically develop first as females and may transition to male later in life, which is common among groupers.

Life expectancy has been reported as up to 40 years in well-maintained conditions, though typical aquarium lifespans may be shorter. Juveniles are often available in the aquarium trade at 5–23 cm.

Color phase: Mottled dark brown to reddish-brown body covered in small iridescent blue dots throughout; posterior portion of body tends to show faint vertical banding. Some individuals display more vivid blue spotting than others.
breedingNotes: Breeding in home aquaria has not been widely documented. This species is a protogynous hermaphrodite — females mature at approximately 22 cm total length and may transition to male. In the wild, dominant males maintain large territories of up to 2000 m². Spawning in captivity is generally considered impractical for home aquaria due to the large space requirements.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, water parameters, tank size, diet, and temperament. Agreed on pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), kH (8–12), and care level (moderate/intermediate). Disagreed on tank size (125 gal vs. LiveAquaria 250 gal) and temperature range (74–82°F vs. 72–78°F). Values averaged per playbook rules.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: The designated secondary URL did not serve a matching species page for Cephalopholis argus specifically; the page returned data for an Argus Grouper but listed adult size as 24 inches (60.96 cm) and minimum tank as 100 gallons (379 L). Adult size of 24 inches aligns with FishBase maximum. Tank size of 100 gal was factored into the average.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (max 60 cm TL, common 40 cm), temperature (24–28°C), native range (Indo-Pacific: Red Sea to French Polynesia), taxonomy (family Epinephelidae, order Perciformes), diet (75–95% fish, some crustaceans), protogynous hermaphrodite status confirmed.

additional.name: Monaco Nature Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed protogynous hermaphrodite status, maximum size 60 cm, lifespan up to 40 years, territorial/solitary behavior, and family Serranidae (note: some sources now use Epinephelidae as the family, split from Serranidae).

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding in home aquaria has not been documented. A protogynous hermaphrodite requiring large territories; home aquarium breeding is generally considered impractical.
